speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape Number 2 of July 11, 1995. Lilian Gordon interviewing Eugene Fried. So, Mr. Fried, you were saying that you were in the brickyard. Were there any other places that the Jewish people were put? Like in a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was-- no, there was another there brick factory-- and a-- a bigger one in the city-- same city, but the other side of the town. So that day when they took us away, my mother went with the-- went with the first transport. And remained-- I went the next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did you know that there was a-- that there were people in the other brickyard?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- oh, we heard about it. They told us about it that there was-- because people, sometimes they went out from the-- from the-- from the ghetto, what you call it. They went to the other one, to the-- you know, they were-- went from one place to another. Sometimes, not just-- they had to go someplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see it yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't see it. No, I didn't see them, no. But when-- when they-- when I went with the second transport, the train went by and so it happened that they slowed down by the train-- second-- the brick factory. And I happened to see my aunt with her little baby-- she was about six months old-- in her hand. But that's the last one I seen them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it her baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was her baby. My uncle-- yeah, my-- it was my-- my cousin. My little cousin. She was about six months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- how were people selected for the first transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were-- they brought the trains-- that's-- and-- [INAUDIBLE]. It was a lot of people. They couldn't take them all at once. A railroad, the train, could only pull so many trains, so many-- so many cars. And it was boxcars. I don't know if you've ever seen the-- the-- the trains that they carry horses, or things like that, closed up freight. They have just a little window on top, and they close the two doors. And over there, they put barbed wired, nobody should be-- even peek out. So we-- I went with the second transport. We were there in the train for days. I don't know, about three. I don't know many days it took us to get to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in your car?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like herring. One on top of each other. It was very put together. But no toilets, no-- nothing. No water. No nothing. They give us nothing. Whatever we brought along, whatever we had, that's all there was the food. No food at all. And a little air was-- that's what came from that window. And a little spot was open like this in the door, and that's all. People used to make right there, urinate, everything. So we got to Birkenau. A lot of people couldn't stand up. They was-- you know, we're laying one on top-- one on top of each other. A lot of them couldn't even walk out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you with your family in the car?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- no. I was-- my-- I saw my mother and my two sisters and my two brothers, they went in the first transport. I remained there with my uncle and the-- the boss I used to work with, the baker. I remained there with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, what, your--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know why it was just [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: --older brother, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older brother-- I told you, they just drafted him. Just the day before they took us away, they drafted him to the labor camp, a Hungarian labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, right. So he was not in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we arrived Birkenau. So we went-- we were a few days without food or any-- anything. When we got to Birkenau, so they got us out of the-- the cars. And people couldn't walk. A lot of people couldn't walk, so they just took them out of the car-- I walked out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I seen our neighbor from downstairs, [INAUDIBLE], he stayed with us there. He was a shoemaker. He couldn't walk because he was-- from the urination on top of each other, they got rashes and everything. They couldn't walk out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they just picked him up like a sack of flour, put him on, like, a shoulder, threw him in a wheelbarrow, and took him on the side there, on the side. A lot of people like that. If they couldn't walk, they just put them right on side. There was [INAUDIBLE]. When-- later I seen what happened. Who picked him up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- also [GERMAN]. They call them-- they could call them [GERMAN]. Other prisoners, Jewish prisoners. But they were there before. You know, the Polish, they were there before us. You know, Polish Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said to us-- we used to want to take something-- don't need it, take it. Look, there's the crematoria was there. They-- that's where they burn the people. They told you that as soon as you got off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, but we asked what that chimney was there. The fire was burning like crazy. So we--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, [INAUDIBLE]. That was Birkenau. So we got there to Birkenau. And people couldn't walk out-- walk down. They just put in wheelbarrows like sack of flour, and they wheeled them over to a-- like a ditch in the side there. They threw them in there. Alive. There was fire-- and it was burning there. Alive. They threw them in the fire just alive. They threw them alive there. And the other side was the-- the-- the crematoriums. Whoever went to the left, like they say-- left and right, left and right. I happened to be in-- on-- they send me to the right. And before-- behind me was a lady with a little baby. I don't know if she smuggled in. It was under her coat jacket. And the German came over, grabbed that little baby-- must have been crying, heard crying-- and grabbed it by the little foot and just threw it away. The little baby. They threw the baby just like-- like-- like-- like nothing. Just picked it up like a ragged doll and threw the baby away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. What could I think? It just-- it just hurts. They took it-- just like, no heart. Like a-- a child. Can't you picture anybody who has a child? A mother from a child, take a baby, grab it by one little leg, and he shows it to you that [INAUDIBLE]. He said look, look. Throws it away. The mother was crying, but she-- she couldn't do nothing. And then they took us in-- [INAUDIBLE]. Then they marched us in, then they separate the ladies from the men. And they went-- they give us showers. Someone who went to-- they tell me showers, the left-hand, they went to the crematoriums.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't know where they're going. They said, we're going to a showers, get showers. And when we finish-- when we-- they took us in, then we marched into the barracks where the showers was. First, it was like mountains-- in a barrack-- they took away all our clothes. They-- everything. The jewelry. There was mountains of jewelry there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And hair on one side. They took off the girls-- the girls' hair. They had beautiful hair, long. Threw them on that side. Jewelry, food, clothing, everything. Mountains of it. It was a big barrack, like, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember I had my first watch. I bough my first watch when I could afford one. They told me, throw it away. I stomped it, and I broke it, then I threw it up there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I threw-- yeah. I stomped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody say anything to you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they couldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you stomped your watch?

speaker: interviewee
text: What could they say? I stomped it. There was so much jewelry, so much gold. And, you know, every-- like a person has their engagement ring, or gold, you know, they took everything away. And then after that, they took us out in our barrack and they shaved us top to bottom. Every piece of hair they shaved off our body.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. They shaved everybody the piece of hair. And they-- then they put some kind of disinfect-- like kerosene, or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: It-- look, it burned like crazy, you know? It burned so much in the-- you know, when you shave, we put a little aftershave, and it hurts, too. That they put kerosene, or something, they put the disinfection or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they gave us the uniforms, those striped uniforms. Just a jacket and a pair of pants. And by luck-- I don't know how I kept my shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- you don't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did other people get? What were they given to wear on their feet?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had like clogs. The wooden shoes-- wooden soles. Not leather, wooden soles. And then, like, sandals and things like that. And then we were in Birkenau. And they were crazy. They were rushing us already over there. Go, go, go. Were beating the hell out of everybody just-- they were still building even barracks then. They were still building barracks-- two by fours. They used to beat people, run, run, run. Go, go, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're there for a couple days, and then they marched us over-- no food. In Birkenau, no food. From there, they-- they gave us those striped uniforms. Those-- Did you-- were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --no underwear.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with any of your family or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only my uncle. My uncle was still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- my-- no, this was the-- the sheet metal worker. And they marched us over to Auschwitz from there. No food in Birkenau. We got to Auschwitz, and a big sign, 'arbeit macht frei.' That means, work will make you free. And a band was playing there. You know, from the-- the prisoners would play in a band. You know, they're talented people. They're playing band.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- What did you think when you-- when you saw this sign and you-- and you heard the band? What did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We didn't know-- we just-- a work camp, you know. Oh, yeah, but when they marched us in-- before they marched us in-- I seen this side, where the two-- where one people went the crematorium on that side, and the other side was a ditch with people-- they threw them-- who couldn't walk. They threw them in the ditch, and they burned them alive there. I don't know if they put kerosene on it, or what. But they just burned them alive. Who couldn't walk, they just burned them alive down there. From the whole train. You know, there was a lot of cars. I don't know how many-- how many there was. How many there was that remained from the people there. Very few. Let's say if there were 10,000, if 2,000 would remain alive there, it was a lot. And then I seen the-- some of the woman came out on the other side-- shaved their head, everything. Also, the same thing with the men. But they were-- but they kept them separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea why they shaved people's hairs?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: The women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Disinfect-- disinfection. But I don't know why they did it. They did it to all the girls and everything. And then when we came to Auschwitz, I remember when we got to Auschwitz, there were, like, two stories-- barracks, but two stories. And we were hungry. And some of the prisoners, you know, they were there a long time already. They threw out-- whoever had a piece of bread, they threw it down. And people were killing each other for a piece of bread. You know, everybody was jumping, running for it. Me or you, whoever could get a piece of bread, grabbed it. But that was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So they finally got back into the barracks-- got the barracks. They settled us in the barracks. They took our clothes away again. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took-- they took so we shouldn't run away, or something. Couldn't run any place. Took our clothes away again, those striped uniforms. And I remember at night they gave us bread. They brought in bread-- counted up exactly how much it is. And there was one slice missing. He counted exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: He went-- he started to yell. He says, the blockalteste. What-- the head of the block. You know, block-- the-- how do you call it? Who's in charge of the-- in charge of there. He said, who took an extra slice of bread? Nobody answered. Finally he went around, he found the guy who took the slice of bread. And there was, like, a stand, you know, like put the head over there? He get him, put him against it, you know? And he had a hose filled with sand, a rubber hose.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave him 25 lashes. He never got up. So he said, that's how it's going-- everybody who's not doing what they're supposed to, going to get 25 like this. And never get up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: He collapsed there. I don't know if he was dead or not. Probably. He never got up. 25 He gave him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was there for three days with clothes. Running around for three days. The third day, that's when they gave us the number. A 4310.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me see it again.

speaker: interviewee
text: There. This was the number. A 4310. That's when it became no more names, just a number. When they write them they-- they registered us. And when they called you, they only called you by the name, A 4310. No more name. We're there for three days, and they gave us uniforms. And my uncle was there. And then, I left. I said goodbye to my uncle. They marched us over to Jawischowitz, another camp. There was a coal mine there. We got over there, put us in a barracks, and there were like, you know, bunks. 15 in a bunk.

speaker: interviewer
text: 15 To 1 bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: 15 to one bunk. It's like, the bunk was about, let's say, it was from here to there, a bunk. It was about, let's say, about six feet wide. In feet, you know. And we used to sleep like one on top of each other. Whatever we could. We used to sleep in there. And then the next day we went to work. Early in the morning. First of all, we had appel. That means reveille over here. Hours. About an hour, at least. And they count, [INAUDIBLE] count. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you have to go outside for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside. Everything outside. And then the-- the camp-- head of the camp used to come out and count. But they count over there. And then the SS came in. They took count, then went to work. And they gave us black coffee. We went to work all day in the coal mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just on coffee? No bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just on coffee. No bread. When we came back at night, they gave us a slice of bread and a soup. If you find a potato, you were lucky. And once in a while they used to give us bloodwurst. It was like a-- like a liverwurst. Like, blood. It was made out of blood. I don't know what they used call it. The German used that a lot-- bloodwurst. And that was for the whole day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn to be a coal miner?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. They-- they made us. It was murder in that coal-- in the coal mine. They used to come around, and they used to chase you. The coal mine, when you-- when you blast coal, is like-- over here is the ground, there is stone, and there is coal. We used to drill and blast it. And then we used to put it on little truck-- little cars, you know, to-- on the railroad there's little tracks-- and shove it out. Then push it out. And the Steiger, you know, used to come. The SS man used to come. He didn't have a regular stick. He used to have an iron stick, a steel-- it was steel-like. Rush, rush, rush, rush. Come on, we need the coal. Rush, rush. You know, he used to beat the hell out of us to rush, to rush.

speaker: interviewer
text: He would go into the coal mine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was-- he was in charge of the coal mine. I think they called him SS Steiger. I used to call him [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to have-- they-- when we [INAUDIBLE], we-- they used to give us a lamp we should be able to see where to go to there. And it was murder. They-- there was one man, he was about six feet tall. Within two weeks, that guy-- he was about six-- that guy couldn't straighten up. He would bend over like this. He couldn't walk anymore. Yeah. And then they used to give coal soap. [SIGHS] They give us soap to wash. It was made-- it was-- [SNIFFLING] it-- it was a yellow soap. [SOBS] it was a yellow soap made of-- [SOBS] it was-- it was-- marks on it, right? RJF. They used to tell us it's Reichs-Juden-Fett, and it was made pure out of Jewish fat.

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us that's what it was made out of. That's what they gave us to wash ourselves. [SOBS] It could have been anybody. Your family. Anybody. [SOBS] And then every morning-- you see-- and then people used to be so sick coming up. Whoever was sick, they used to pay them a single [INAUDIBLE]. One day, I can remember, I came home from work, and my boss, you know, the one who used to work in the bakery, he wasn't-- he was carrying mattresses that day for the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was carrying what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mattresses--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, mattresses.

speaker: interviewee
text: --For the SS. And he got a herniated stomach from it. They put him in the barrack. I went to see him that day. And the next time-- the next day, they took him away the Auschwitz. Never. Whoever went to a sick hall never came back. If we put him into that room-- they couldn't work-- never came back. Straight to Auschwitz. I remember, I went for-- my ear was bothering me. Was running, my ear. I went into a sick hall, and there was two doctors there. And they took out a wire, and they put a piece of cotton on the-- on it. And he just dug into my ear, broke the wall of my ear, and I was yelling. And they were laughing. And it was-- was broken. It make-- made a water-- a-- a drain of something. I don't know. And since, I never been back to-- never been back to sick hall. And it still bothers me, my ear. That's why I can't hear on it even.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lost the hearing in that ear?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost the hearing in that ear because-- they-- and it was whoever went to that sick hall, and they-- every once in a week they used to come with a truck. They put them on a truck to Auschwitz, never came back. It was the-- straight to crematoriums.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you didn't go to sick hall after you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no way. If I even had a cold, anything, never went. But if they did, anybody who did went to sick hall, never came out of there. They came with a truck every so and often, and they took them to Auschwitz. Never came back out of the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you work in the coal mine?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the coal mines till January.

speaker: interviewer
text: January, 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, 1945. And I remembered once, they brought a transport from the prison. They emptied out the prison. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they emptied out the prison from Hungary. And they-- they came.

speaker: interviewer
text: They figured they'd be able to escape. So some of them escaped. They brought them back, and when we came back from work, we know-- they put everybody outside to watch. And they had those-- what do you call those? Nooses? Where they hang people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody had to come outside and watch. Put them on that. Hung them on there, pull the string, and hang them right there. They said, that's what's going to happen if anybody escapes. They hung them right there. Everybody had to watch it-- till they-- till they hung them. When they were dead, they didn't cut them down. They were completely dead and [INAUDIBLE], just cut it down-- drop. They let them fall on the ground-- the floor-- on that floor. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: A matter of fact, I knew him just because I was working together with him. But otherwise, I wouldn't have known. You know, just-- they came from, that time, the Hungarian transport. Wasn't Jewish. They were from prison. You know, they emptied out the prisons.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a lot of non-Jews, too, in there. Matter of fact, the one barrack was a lot of Christians. They were, like, cooks and kapos. You know what a kapo is? Kapo is, like, was in charge, you know? And the day before they evacuated us, they disappeared. Where did they go, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard they took them to Auschwitz and they killed them before they had a chance to escape, because they-- they probably-- there were a lot of from Poland. They probably knew their way around. So they said they took them and they killed the before they had the chance to escape. But they-- but they didn't care all that. People were just like animals, they treated them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- how were the kapos to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you, the kapos, some of them were-- most of them were like German-- the criminals, you know? And they-- they used to-- what you call it-- beat the hell out of somebody, too, if they didn't do what they want. But they were in charge, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember, like, we used to dig ditches in the camp, and they used to have fun chasing the people in the ditch, in and out, with swings-- with-- what do you-- what-- how do you call it? This? Whips?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whips. They used to [? Beat them ?] just to run around. And they're fun-- the SS used to sit on top and laugh. Oh, do it again. Do it again. Just laughing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever--

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, they must--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever in that situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was just once-- what do you call-- and-- a remember I got hit once there with the [INAUDIBLE]. They used to bring in the SS food for the SS. And they brought back the empty containers, and everybody was running to get it just to lick it up. You know, was soup in there. There was thick soup. Just what's left [INAUDIBLE]. And I ran out the camp to the gate to get it from the truck. And the SS came with that rubber hose, remember that-- the-- what they filled with sand. He give me a smack on my back, and I never run again. Never did run out again for anything like this. People used to go-- they were so tired of that. They used to run against the wires, the barbed wires. They were electric. They used to ran. They did. They got so tired of living, they ran to the barbed wires and they killed-- they got-- they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make any friends in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We had friends. Just kids, you know, between our ages and things like that. You were just talking friends. We were-- how could you make-- just-- just same thing, working with us. There were a lot of different-- they were from all kind-- Hungary, Romania, and Greece. From all over. Matter of fact, there were a lot of kids from Romania. Not Jews, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were they in prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their parents probably said a bad word, or something, against the regime.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so they were in camp-- in the camp, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in January of '45, what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: January '45, 19-- 1945. 15, then when the-- the invasion, the Russian invasion, started. Then they start to evacuate us. Matter of fact, we've seen outside the camp go and-- another camp. Must have been-- I think, was Buna. Another camp also from there. They evacuated. And then the next day, we started to march. It was January. It was cold. It was-- over in Europe, not a cold like here. It was so cold. And the deep-- the snow was very deep over there. We were marching over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you still have your shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I still had my shoes. I still had my shoes. No socks, because they didn't give you no socks or underwear. Just that thin striped uniform with a cap. That's all. Just like pajamas. Thin like pajamas. Nothing-- it was-- if you wear pajamas to bed, that's what it was. Nothing else. No underwear, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: People were dying. They were marching in that snow. I remember when we were marching, we stopped in a hay barn for a while. A barn, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: There they give us bread and some soup, old-- from all this time. And no water, because [INAUDIBLE] snow. So for the drink, we drank some-- we had some snow. We melted snow down. We were there overnight. Then we went to a train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how many people were in the barn, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember how many people, because a lot-- some people didn't go in. Matter-- by the time we got to that barn, a lot of people got killed because anybody who remained behind-- three feet-- they just shot them right there and there in the gutters. They left them there. Three feet. That's no [INAUDIBLE]. Whoever could not walk, they cut them-- they shot them right down there. There was an SS behind them. Every three feet there was an SS. They were all [INAUDIBLE], but you couldn't do nothing because they had the machine guns.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whoever remained three feet behind the lines, they put-- they put them down, give them a bullet in their head. And we-- they left them right there. So we got there, so-- Did you have a partner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friend-- no such thing as partner. We're friends-- they were-- you know, acquaintance, you know, from the camps, you know. And when we got there, we took a train to Buchenwald from over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What town did you get to? Did you march to from Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was Leipzig from there. I think it was Leipzig. I don't remember exactly. I think it was Leipzig. That's how we-- we get-- that-- we stayed overnight there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any German people see you know marching? Did anybody say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't say nothing. They seen, but didn't say nothing. Absolutely nothing. And-- if-- if you find a piece of grass or something from under the snow, we ate it. Or something. A potato. Anything we find, you know, we ate, because you were starved for days. You know, no food at all. And then from over there, we finally got a train. And we got to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many miles do you think you walked?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It was for days we marched. For days. They finally got us on the train to Buchenwald. And there also-- in Buchenwald there also, 'arebeit macht frei.' Same thing. And over there it was murder. They put us in that barrack. And also about 15 in one-- like herrings. They used to slept. And in the mornings, they used to come-- they didn't wake you. They come with their sticks, beat you out of the trunk-- out-- out of the bunk. Out, out. For appel. 5 O'clock in the morning. And it was cold. We stood there for hours in the cold. People were just collapsing and die in the snow there. And then about three, four, hours later, because we didn't go to work over there too much, in Buchenwald. About five-- three or four hours later, the SS came and they asked if everything was all right. So we went back in the barracks for a while. And then, in the-- after that, they took us out to work for, like, breaking up stones for the highways, things like that. Things-- you know, just-- just to keep us busy. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage to get up every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: They made us get up every day. They beat-- they beat you. They chased you out with the sticks to get up. And you would-- you know, you get so numb. If they hit you, you don't even feel it. They don't even feel it, they hit you like this. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you get-- you get numb. I never cried in concentration camp. Never. It was just like-- I never-- I never shed a tear. It was so-- you get so numb. You don't even know what's going on. You just know one thing you have to do. It's just-- matter of fact, in Buchenwald, when I see what they do with American prisoners, too. Prisoners of war. Because I remember there was American prisoner with-- with his-- they had, the, you know, this-- the-- the army clothes on yet. And [INAUDIBLE] there was this American one, was a black guy. They beat the hell out him. They said, go, you black so-and-so. And in German you say, schwarz hund, schwarz hund. Go, you know, black-- they used to call him black-- black dog. Go, go. They used to hit him. He was pulling-- pushing a wheelbarrow. And the-- and the white guy also. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were talking, you know. They-- they won't talk to each other, but the Germans were yelling at them, [GERMAN], you American, swine. You know? That's why I know they were Americans. And he was black, because in Europe there was no black people, so that's why I knew he was American.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were in Buchenwald for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get to talk to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we couldn't talk to them. [SIGHS] And from Buchenwald-- I was there for a while-- we went to Reinsdorf. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was-- I don't know how many miles from-- from Theresienstadt. We marched over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, you said you went to Reinsdorf.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from Buchenwald. They took us to Reinsdorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you march there? Or did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We marched there, from Buchenwald. And the same thing-- march. Whoever died on the street, just left them there. And we were working, and over there was the barracks, and also same thing. Barracks, same thing. No food, no water. Nothing. At night they used to give you, like, potato soup or something like that. Very seldom they used to give a piece of bread. And we used to go to work. There was a Zeiss factory-- the-- I think it was a Zeiss factory. That's what they used to tell us.

speaker: interviewer
text: The lens--

speaker: interviewee
text: The lens factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: The lens company?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But it was not producing, because they bombed it. And we just rebuild it on one floor. We used to carry the big machines and everything like this. And that's what-- that's what we used to-- [INAUDIBLE]. They got about-- but it didn't help any, because at night the Russians came. They bombed it, again. They used to destroy it all the time. But we still used to go clean up, and work and-- and-- and work at it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was in Reinsdorf.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Reinsdorf. And that camp was terrible. They-- people used to die over there. They used to-- it was like every day they used to go with a-- you ever see a banana pushcart? They used to go pick up the people from the-- from the barracks, dead people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They have that, throw them in the pushcart. And they empty them on, like, a mountain. Ever see a mountain of dirt when they-- when they build? They used to throw the corpse on top of the mountain. And That's How they used to dump people.
